title: Writing on fabric with calligraphy ink
materials: pre-washed cotton or linen fabric (tightly woven, without sizing)
fabric calligraphy ink or iron-fix ink (e.g. DecoArt, or calligraphy-specific fabric ink)
craft foam or felt pad under the fabric (prevents nib catching on weave)
masking tape for stretching fabric taut
sources: SYNTHESISED — fabric calligraphy technique. Draws on general calligraphy ink chemistry (gum arabic binders, iron-gall properties) documented in Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain.
notes: Writing calligraphy letterforms on fabric with a dip pen and fabric ink. The challenge: fabric is not paper; the nib catches on the weave. Solutions: stretch the fabric taut, place a soft mat underneath, use a broader nib than you would on paper (Mitchell 2 or 3 instead of 0), and use fabric-specific ink rather than iron-gall (which can be acid on some fabrics). Covers: fabric preparation (wash to remove sizing, iron flat, tape taut to a board), ink selection (water-soluble vs heat-fix), nib choice, the different feel of writing on fabric vs paper (more drag, need slower speed), heat-setting for permanence. What works: broad-edge Foundational and Italic on tightly woven cotton. What fails: Copperplate on loosely woven fabric.
